methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $1.6B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 8 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing. Range: $300M - $10.0B.

private.name: Nimble Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B

data.narrative:
Hadrian, a Series C robotics and manufacturing company founded by Chris Power, is valued at $1.6B post-money following $560M in cumulative fundraising. Valuation analysis relies on a peer set of eight private robotics companies at the same or adjacent stage, supplemented by one public comparable.

Two triangulation methods apply here. Stage-Progression analysis, anchoring to the $1.6B last post-money valuation, applies a 1.7x multiplier typical of Series C-to-next-stage advancement, implying $2.7B. Peer-Median analysis across the eight-company robotics cohort—including Nimble Robotics ($1.0B Series C), Exotec ($2.0B Series D), and Bedrock Robotics ($1.8B Series B)—yields a median valuation of $1.3B, with a range spanning $300M to $10.0B. The wide dispersion reflects material stage and business-model heterogeneity within the robotics sector.

Synthesizing both methods produces a triangulated range of $1.1B (low) to $3.1B (high), with a median of $2.0B. The current $1.6B valuation sits in the lower-middle portion of this range, suggesting limited downside but meaningful upside if stage progression and operational execution track peer experience. Key uncertainties include capital efficiency relative to comparables, path to profitability, and the elevated volatility inherent in early-stage hardware capital requirements.
